source_alignment.subject: Science
source_alignment.source_statement: The particulate nature of matter, including the properties of solids, liquids and gases, and changes of state in terms of the particle model.
source_alignment.derived_objective_note: This pack bridges KS2 to KS3 science by making particle models interactive, explanatory and misconception-aware.

objective.child_goal: I can use particles to explain solids, liquids and gases.
objective.statement: Use the particle model to describe properties of solids, liquids and gases and explain changes of state.
misconceptions: Thinks particles in a solid do not move at all.
Thinks particles themselves expand when a substance is heated.
Thinks melting destroys particles rather than changing their arrangement and movement.

vocabulary.child_explanation: A tiny piece that materials are made from.
vocabulary.audio_script: Particles are tiny pieces that make up substances.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: Small dots making a model of a material.

vocabulary.child_explanation: Whether a material is a solid, liquid or gas.
vocabulary.audio_script: A state tells us whether the substance is solid, liquid or gas.

vocabulary.child_explanation: When a solid changes into a liquid.
vocabulary.audio_script: Melting happens when particles gain energy and move out of fixed positions.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: Ordered particles becoming able to slide past each other.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Sort these properties into solid, liquid or gas.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Reactivate observable properties before particle explanation.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: First sort what we can observe, then we will explain it with particles.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Property cards move into three labelled chambers.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Zoom in to see particles in each state.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Introduce particle models as explanations, not literal photographs.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: A model helps us explain what is happening, even when particles are too small to see.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: A zoom lens transitions from material sample to particle model.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Compare how particles are arranged and moving in each state.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: Solid particles vibrate in fixed positions. Liquid particles move past each other. Gas particles are far apart and move freely.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Three particle chambers animate with different spacing and movement.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Raise the energy and watch the solid melt.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Connect heating to particle movement and arrangement changes.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: When particles gain energy, they move more and may change arrangement.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: An energy slider changes particle movement from solid to liquid.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: When a solid melts, what happens to the particles?
teaching_sequence.audio_script: The particles do not disappear. Their arrangement and movement change.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Particle count stays the same while arrangement changes.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Teach Lyra why solid particles still move.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Repair the no-motion-in-solids misconception through explanation.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: I thought solid particles were frozen still. Can you show me the vibration?
teaching_sequence.visual_model: The companion pauses a solid model and asks for evidence.

manipulatives.purpose: Let learners manipulate energy and observe particle spacing, arrangement and movement in each state.
manipulatives.interaction_notes: Learners adjust energy, compare chambers, freeze frames, sort models and explain changes of state.
manipulatives.accessibility_notes: Particle movement has reduced-motion and static-frame alternatives; descriptions are available as audio and text.

manipulatives.purpose: Make model evaluation strategic by asking learners to select, critique and improve particle representations using observable evidence.
manipulatives.interaction_notes: Learners compare freeze frames, sort models by state or energy, flag impossible features and revise one model before defending the change.
manipulatives.accessibility_notes: Uses static particle frames, texture and spacing labels, keyboard sorting, audio descriptions and a motion-free comparison table.

practice.interleaving_notes: Interleave state identification, model sorting and change-of-state explanation after particle vocabulary is secure.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Match particle arrangements to solids, liquids and gases.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: particle diagram + state labels + property clues
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Diagrams must communicate spacing and arrangement without implying real scale.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Compare movement in solids, liquids and gases.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: three chambers + speed controls + movement observation prompt
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Solid vibration should be visible but gentle in low-sensory mode.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Explain heating and cooling using particle arrangement and movement.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: energy slider + starting state + predict final state
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Particle size should remain constant to avoid expansion misconception.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Choose explanations that preserve particles while changing arrangement and movement.
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Language must distinguish substance change from particle identity.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Maintain state model vocabulary and explanations through spaced review.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: mixed model, property and energy prompts
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Use short tasks suitable for daily warm-up orbit.

body.prompt: Which sentence best describes particles in a solid?
choices: They vibrate in fixed positions
They do not move at all
They are far apart and move freely
They disappear until the solid melts

expected_answer.value: They vibrate in fixed positions

hints: Solid particles stay arranged, but they are not completely still.
Look for the tiny vibration in the model.
question_variants.explanation: Particles in a solid vibrate in fixed positions.

body.prompt: Raise the energy until the solid melts. What changes?
choices: Particles move more and can slide past each other
Particles get much bigger
Particles disappear
Particles stop moving

expected_answer.value: Particles move more and can slide past each other

hints: Watch the movement, not the particle size.
Count the particles before and after.
question_variants.explanation: During melting, particles gain energy, move more and change arrangement; the particles are not destroyed.

body.prompt: Which model shows a gas?
choices: A
B
C

expected_answer.value: model_with_far_apart_random_particles

hints: Gas particles are far apart.
Gas particles move freely and fill the container.
question_variants.explanation: A gas model shows particles far apart, moving freely and filling the container.

body.prompt: Model set 1: which chamber represents a solid?
choices: A
B
C
body.supported_interaction: Use labelled touch or keyboard controls. Equivalent routes include single-switch scanning, eye-gaze dwell selection, AAC, pointing, partner or adult-supported response; fine dragging, handwriting and speech are never required.

expected_answer.value: model_with_close_fixed_particles

hints: Look for particles that are close together in fixed positions.
In a solid, particles vibrate.
question_variants.explanation: A solid model shows particles close together in fixed positions; they vibrate.

feedback.correct: Correct. A solid model shows particles close together in fixed positions; they vibrate.
feedback.retry: Try again calmly: Look for particles that are close together in fixed positions. In a solid, particles vibrate. No progress is lost for checking or changing your answer.
feedback.repair: Repair the solid particles do not move route: Look for particles that are close together in fixed positions. Then use the evidence to explain your choice.
feedback.misconception_check: Check the solid particles do not move idea against the task evidence.
feedback.support_message: Touch, keyboard, switch, eye-gaze, AAC, pointing and adult-supported routes are valid; speed, speech and fine-motor precision are not scored.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Thinks particles in a solid do not move at all.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: Learner selects no movement for solid particles.
repair_sequence: Zoom into a solid and show small vibration only.
Compare vibration with free gas movement.
Ask learner to describe fixed position and movement together.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: Learner states that solid particles vibrate in fixed positions.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Thinks particles themselves expand when a substance is heated.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: Learner explains heating by saying particles get bigger.
repair_sequence: Lock particle size during the energy-slider animation.
Show spacing and speed changing instead.
Ask learner to compare particle size before and after.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: Learner explains heating using movement and spacing, not particle size.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Thinks melting destroys particles rather than changing their arrangement and movement.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: Learner says particles disappear or become liquid particles.
repair_sequence: Count particles before and after melting.
Show the same particles changing arrangement.
Ask learner to choose the explanation that preserves particles.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: Learner explains melting as a change in arrangement and movement.

adaptive_support.low_sensory: Use calm lab colours and avoid rapid particle movement by default.
adaptive_support.reduced_motion: Provide static snapshots at low, medium and high energy with optional slow playback.
adaptive_support.audio_first: Narrate particle model observations and allow replay of vocabulary cards.
adaptive_support.reading_support: Use diagrams, icons and short explanation chunks before dense text.
adaptive_support.attention_support: Keep simulation controls limited to one slider or sort action per step.
adaptive_support.processing_support: Allow freeze-frame inspection and side-by-side comparisons.
adaptive_support.confidence_support: Use model-testing language so misconceptions become hypotheses to check.
adaptive_support.eal_support: Preteach particle, solid, liquid, gas, vibrate, arrangement, energy, melting and evaporating.

evidence.mastery_rule: Expected requires matching particle models to states; secure requires explaining changes of state using arrangement, movement and energy.
evidence.parent_summary: Can use particles to explain solids, liquids, gases and changes such as melting.
evidence.teacher_evidence: Uses the particle model accurately, including solid vibration, gas spacing and change-of-state explanations.
next_actions: Connect particle model to density and diffusion after state explanations are secure.
Use graph and energy transfer links for KS3 progression.
Add retrieval tasks that require both diagram choice and written explanation.

qa.notes: Year 7 particle flagship reaches the 240-item pilot target with three preserved curated variants, the complete established generated bank, and deterministic mission candidates spanning particle models, state changes, conservation, diffusion, temperature and energy, evidence, misconceptions and model limitations. Every generated model preserves particle count and particle size and provides supported SEND interactions, static and reduced-motion alternatives, and rich low-pressure feedback; review candidates still require independent science-teacher, accessibility, safeguarding and simulation-usability review before promotion.

accessibility_policy.no_timer_or_penalty: Learners are never timed for mastery and never lose earned progress for mistakes; incorrect attempts route to calm repair, replay and retry.
accessibility_policy.equivalent_response_routes: Every child-facing task must preserve an equivalent route through touch, keyboard, switch, eye-gaze, AAC, pointing, partner-mediated or adult-scribed response where appropriate.
accessibility_policy.audio_equivalent: Audio and read-aloud support must complement visible text, visuals and replay. Child-facing narration must use produced, human-listening-approved assets before release; browser TTS is not a teaching or phonics fallback.
accessibility_policy.reduced_motion: Reduced-motion and static alternatives must preserve the same curriculum evidence, feedback and reward meaning without relying on animation.
